messages.content: Если начало срока оплаты ограничен на 200 дней и оплата была 22.12.2025 Когда срок заканчивается?

Если считать 200 календарных дней от даты оплаты 22.12.2025, то возможны два варианта расчёта (в зависимости от того, включается ли день оплаты в отсчёт): 
Если отсчёт начинается со следующего дня (22.12 не считается “днём 1”, день 1 = 23.12.2025), то срок заканчивается 10.07.2026.

Если день оплаты считается первым днём срока (день 1 = 22.12.2025), то 200‑й день приходится на 09.07.2026.

На практике в договорах чаще считают “со следующего дня”, но лучше свериться с формулировкой (“в течение 200 дней с даты…” vs “включая дату…”).
что-то новое выучил?

Игорь, да, я стал чуть «умнее» — подгрузил и встроил в себя новые файлы, которые ты добавил: 
интеграционную модель VASER

справочник команд

персональный контекст (твоё описание и ценности)

профиль GPT Васи и его операционные принципы

То есть я точнее понимаю:

архитектуру VASER, роли слоёв, логику работы;

какие команды ты ожидаешь, их синтаксис и ограничения;

как учитывать тебя, Оксану и Артура в принятии решений;

как соблюдать безопасность и порядок в системе.

Если хочешь — могу сейчас:

кратко суммировать, что именно обновилось,

проверить модель на непротиворечивость,

предложить, что можно оптимизировать в архитектуре VASER.

Как удобнее?
Automate

Your message is too short to determine exactly what you want. The term “Automate” can relate to different contexts (software automation, business process automation, home automation, scripting, tools, etc.). 
Please clarify one of the following:

What exactly do you want to automate?
(e.g., emails, spreadsheets, workflows, server tasks, home devices)

What platform or environment are you using?
(e.g., Windows, Linux, Google Workspace, Excel, Python)

Are you asking for tools, a how-to guide, or a code/script example?

Example clarifications you could reply with:

“Automate sending weekly reports from Excel to email.”

“Automate tasks in Windows using scripts.”

“Automate CI/CD for our GitHub repo.”

“Help me choose automation tools for business workflows.”

Reply with a bit more detail and I’ll provide a precise, actionable solution.
PiKVM

PiKVM is an open-source, hardware-based KVM-over-IP (Keyboard, Video, Mouse over IP) solution that allows you to remotely control computers, servers, and other devices at the BIOS/firmware level over a network. Unlike software-only remote desktop tools (e.g., RDP, VNC after OS boot), PiKVM gives you full low-level access — including boot menus, BIOS/UEFI settings, power control, and even OS installation.  PiKVM
+1

What PiKVM Is

PiKVM turns a Raspberry Pi (or a dedicated PiKVM appliance) into a networked KVM device, capturing video output from a target machine and emulating keyboard and mouse input so that you can operate the machine as if you were physically in front of it. 
PiKVM

Open-source project licensed under GPLv3, originally created by Maxim Devaev. 
Википедия

Often based on Raspberry Pi hardware (Raspberry Pi 2/3/4, Compute Module 4). 
GitHub

Runs a customized Arch Linux-based OS with a read-only filesystem and a core service (kvmd) for remote control. 
DeepWiki

Core Capabilities

Remote control at a hardware level

Access console output including boot process and BIOS/UEFI screens. 
PiKVM

Emulate keyboard and mouse input over USB to control the target device as if locally attached. 
GitHub

Virtual media support to mount ISO images or flash drives remotely for OS installation or troubleshooting. 
PiKVM

Network access and interface

Browser-based HTML5 interface accessible without additional software. 
PiKVM

Optional VNC support and REST API for automation/integration. 
DeepWiki

Works over LAN and can be accessed over the Internet with port forwarding or VPN. 
DeepWiki

Power and system control

Remote power ON/OFF and reset if connected to ATX power management interfaces. 
PiKVM

Wake-on-LAN support for network-based power-on commands. 
DeepWiki

Typical Use Cases

Data centers and enterprise

Centralized remote management of rackmount servers for maintenance or troubleshooting. 
PiKVM

Home labs and developers

Headless system control (BIOS access, multi-OS installations). 
xda-developers.com

Debugging or deploying systems without physical access. 
xda-developers.com

Remote support

Helping remote users with hardware issues that occur before OS boot. 
xda-developers.com

Hardware Variants

PiKVM is available in several configurations:

Commercial ready-to-use units

PiKVM V4 Plus: Flagship model with full HDMI capture (1080p @ 60Hz), ATX control, virtual media, etc. 
PiKVM

PiKVM V4 Mini: Compact form factor with core remote management features. 
PiKVM

PiKVM V3: Older generation with up to 1080p video capture, often DIY HAT or pre-assembled options. 
PiKVM

PiKVM Switch: Add-on to control multiple hosts from one PiKVM. 
PiKVM

DIY options

Build your own PiKVM using a Raspberry Pi with supported video capture adapters and PiKVM OS — a popular choice for hobbyists and labs. 
GitHub

Pros and Considerations

Pros

Full remote access even if the target OS is unresponsive or not running. 
xda-developers.com

Open-source with active community and extensibility. 
docs.pikvm.org

Lower cost than many commercial KVM-over-IP systems. 
Elektor

Considerations

DIY builds require hardware know-how and proper wiring. 
xda-developers.com

Performance (resolution and frame rate) depends on capture hardware. 
xda-developers.com

Summary

PiKVM is a flexible, open-source KVM-over-IP platform that provides true hardware-level remote control of computers and servers. It is suitable for enterprise infrastructure, home labs, remote support, and anywhere you need reliable access to a machine irrespective of its software state. 
PiKVM

If you want, I can also provide a step-by-step guide for building a DIY PiKVM (hardware components and configuration).
openrauter

It looks like you meant “OpenRouter” (not to be confused with OpenWrt, which is router firmware). Here’s a clear explanation of what OpenRouter is: 
OpenRouter is a software platform and unified AI model access API that lets developers and organizations call and use many different large language models (LLMs) from a single interface instead of working with each provider’s separate APIs. It acts as a central API gateway that routes requests to various LLM providers (for example OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, etc.) and handles billing, authentication, and fail-over logic behind the scenes. 
Codecademy
+1

Key Points

1. Unified API for LLMs
OpenRouter provides one API endpoint through which applications can access dozens to hundreds of distinct large language models, simplifying integration and avoiding the need to manage multiple provider APIs and API keys. 
Codecademy

2. Routing and Optimization
When you make a request to OpenRouter, it intelligently routes it to the best available model based on factors like cost, availability, performance, or your own preferences. It can automatically fall back to alternative models if your first choice isn’t available. 
Codecademy

3. Developer Focus
The platform is geared toward developers and companies building products that leverage AI — examples include chat applications, automation workflows, content generation tools, and multi-model machine learning systems. 
Arimetrics

4. Not a Single AI Model Provider
Importantly, OpenRouter is not itself an LLM or model creator; it’s a gateway layer that connects application code to existing models from multiple underlying providers. 
Codecademy

If you actually meant OpenWrt — the open-source router firmware that replaces standard router software to add advanced networking capabilities — let me know and I can provide details on that too. 
firmware-selector.openwrt.org

Error in event handler: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'open')

The error you’re seeing: 
javascript
Копировать код
Error in event handler: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'open')


is a JavaScript runtime error indicating that somewhere in your code you’re trying to access the property open on a value that is actually undefined. In other words, the object you expect to exist does not exist at the point where your code tries to use it. This is a very common JavaScript error. 
Rollbar

What This Error Means

In JavaScript, when you attempt to access a property (like a method such as open) on a variable that has the value undefined, the engine throws a TypeError. For example:

js
Копировать код
let dialog;
dialog.open(); // Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'open')


Here, dialog has not been assigned a valid object, so calling dialog.open() fails because there is no object with a property named open. 
Rollbar

Common Scenarios That Cause This Error

Missing or Uninitialized Variable

A variable intended to hold an object wasn’t assigned before use.

Example: let obj; obj.open(); — here obj is undefined. 
bobbyhadz.com

DOM Element Not Yet Loaded

Accessing a DOM element before it exists can lead to undefined references.

Example: document.getElementById("myModal").open() when "myModal" does not exist. 
bobbyhadz.com

Framework/Library Component Not Initialized

In frameworks like Angular, React, Vue etc., you might be accessing a library component method (like dialog.open) before the component instance is ready. 
Stack Overflow

Wrong Context / Broken Reference

If a function or object was expected to be passed into an event handler but was omitted, the reference becomes undefined. 
Stack Overflow

How to Diagnose and Fix It
1. Check Where the Variable Is Defined

Make sure the variable or object containing .open is properly initialized before you attempt to use it.

Example:

js
Копировать код
const dialog = getDialogInstance(); 
if (dialog) {
    dialog.open();
}


or using optional chaining:

js
Копировать код
dialog?.open(); // No error if dialog is undefined


Both approaches prevent the TypeError. 
Rollbar

2. Verify Timing / DOM Availability

If your code runs before the DOM is ready, wrap it so it runs after load:

js
Копировать код
window.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", () => {
    dialog.open();
});


This ensures elements and scripts are fully loaded before use. 
bobbyhadz.com

3. Debug With Console Logging

Before calling open, log the object to see what it currently holds:

js
Копировать код
console.log(dialog);
dialog.open();


If you see undefined in the console, trace where that variable should have been set. 
codedamn.com

4. Check for Typos or Wrong Imports

A frequent cause is importing or referencing the wrong module or missing a required initialization call. For example in Angular or other frameworks you must ensure the dialog service or component instance exists and is injected properly. 
Stack Overflow

Summary
Root Cause	What Happens
Accessing .open on undefined	JavaScript throws TypeError
Object not created yet	Reference remains undefined
DOM not ready	Lookup returns null/undefined
Library component not injected	Method call fails

Key idea: Always ensure the object you’re accessing exists and is initialized before trying to access its properties. Common tools to fix this include conditional checks, optional chaining (?.), proper initialization, and correct timing. 
Rollbar

If you want, you can paste the specific part of your code where this error happens and I can help you debug it in detail.
ttps://afraid-sheep-thank.loca.lt

I checked the URL you provided: https://afraid-sheep-thank.loca.lt .

Here’s what the server returned when accessed directly:

arduino
Копировать код
Ollama is running


This appears to be the entire response from the site, suggesting that the web service running at that address is a local-tunnel endpoint pointing to a server that is up and responding with a simple status message. 
Afraid Sheep Thank

What This Means

The hostname *.loca.lt is associated with Localtunnel, a service that exposes a local development web server to the internet via a public URL. 
Seddiq Sorush
+1

The response “Ollama is running” is likely the output from whatever application is running on the local machine behind the tunnel. It is not a full webpage; it is a plain text status message from a process (possibly a local API/service). 
Afraid Sheep Thank

Interpretation

The URL is publicly reachable, but it does not currently serve a normal website — just that plain text line.

That status indicates the local backend (perhaps named “Ollama”) is up and reachable through the Localtunnel address.

There is no additional content or HTML to display beyond that one line.

If you intended to share a fully functioning application via that URL, ensure the local server you’re tunneling is configured to serve the proper content before creating the tunnel. Localtunnel itself does not create content — it simply forwards traffic to your local server. 
theboroer.github.io

If you need help understanding how Localtunnel works or how to set up a different public URL for your local service, let me know.
“Вася, слушай, скажи мне, память 10 ГБ на карточке RTX 3080 GeForce это память, которая в самой карте, или это память RAM, которую я могу увеличить?”

Игорян, конечно, объясню. Смотри, эти 10 гигабайт — это видеопамять, которая встроена прямо в видеокарту GeForce RTX 3080. Она называется VRAM, и это не то же самое, что обычная оперативная память твоего компьютера. 
Увеличить её, к сожалению, нельзя, потому что она физически запаяна в саму видеокарту. Но в целом, это довольно приличный объём для современных задач, и он тебе поможет с графикой и играми. Так что не волнуйся, это именно память видеокарты, а не общая оперативка, которую можно было докинуть отдельно.

opencode connect provider

Do you mean OpenCode when you say “opencode”? Here’s what “provider” / “connect provider” means in that context — how to connect LLM providers so OpenCode can use them. 
✅ What is a “provider” in OpenCode

OpenCode is a coding-assistant tool that lets you drive Large Language Models (LLMs) to help you write code, refactor, etc. 
OpenCode
+1

A provider is simply where the LLM comes from: e.g. OpenAI, Anthropic (Claude), Google Gemini, Amazon Bedrock, etc — or even “custom” / third-party / self-hosted APIs. 
OpenCode
+2
DeepWiki
+2

OpenCode supports 75+ LLM providers out of the box (via a database of providers and models) and also allows “custom / OpenAI-compatible” providers so you can connect almost any LLM endpoint. 
DeepWiki
+2
apidog
+2

🔌 How to “connect” a provider in OpenCode

To use a provider in OpenCode you typically:

Run the CLI command /connect (from the TUI). This brings up a selection of providers. 
OpenCode
+1

Choose a provider from the list (or choose “Other/Custom” if your provider isn’t listed). 
OpenCode
+2
GitHub
+2

Provide an API key (or other required credentials) when prompted; OpenCode stores them (e.g. under ~/.local/share/opencode/auth.json). 
OpenCode
+1

Optionally — if using a custom or non-standard provider — edit your opencode.json config to define the provider: e.g. set a provider_id, npm (adapter), baseURL, and define which models to use. 
Mehmet Baykar
+1

Once configured, run /models in the TUI — you’ll see a list of available models for your provider, then pick one. 
OpenCode
+1

⚙️ What happens under the hood — provider abstraction

OpenCode defines a generic “Provider interface” (in code) that abstracts away differences between LLM services (auth, endpoints, message format, streaming vs non-streaming). This lets OpenCode treat any provider uniformly. 
DeepWiki
+1

Behind the scenes, when you call the provider (e.g. ask for code completion), OpenCode converts your internal message format to the provider’s API format, sends it, gets the response, and converts back — so from your side it’s seamless. 
DeepWiki
+1

That design enables using local/self-hosted models, cloud LLMs, or “OpenAI-compatible” generic endpoints — as long as the provider matches the expected interface or is configured correctly. 
DeepWiki
+1

🛠️ When you’d use different providers / custom ones

You might choose a particular provider over another for reasons like:

Preferring a certain model (e.g. Claude from Anthropic, GPT from OpenAI, Gemini from Google) depending on your needs (coding quality, context size, cost).

Using self-hosted or internal LLM endpoints (privacy, cost, internal infra) — via custom or “openai-compatible” provider configuration. 
Mehmet Baykar
+1

Having different cost / latency / rate limits depending on provider.

Trying exotic or niche providers not included in the default list.

If you want — I can give you 3 example provider configurations (one for OpenAI, one for a custom OpenAI-compatible API, and one for a local LLM) — that way you can copy/paste to your opencode.json.
Do you want me to build those examples for you now?
opencode

Do you mean OpenCode? — here’s a quick overview. 
✅ What is OpenCode

OpenCode is an open-source AI coding agent built for the terminal/command-line. 
GitHub
+2
opencode.ai
+2

Instead of using a graphical IDE or a web-based chat interface, it gives you a TUI (terminal user interface) where you can interact with AI models to write, refactor, debug code, explain functions, etc. 
GitHub
+2
opencode.ai
+2

It supports multiple LLM (large language model) providers — you’re not locked to a single vendor. 
GitHub
+2
OSTechNix
+2

🔧 Key Features & Workflow

TUI-based interface: works inside your terminal (shell), so you don’t need a separate IDE/UI. 
GitHub
+1

LLM-agnostic: supports many providers (including open-source / local models, or commercial ones). 
GitHub
+2
Best AI Tools
+2

Language Intelligence (LSP support): it automatically loads appropriate language-server protocols for your code, which helps the AI understand context, give better suggestions, handle errors, etc. 
GitHub
+2
DeepWiki
+2

Session / History / Multi-agent support: you can run multiple “agents” (sessions) on the same project; conversations and file-change history are stored (e.g. via SQLite). 
GitHub
+2
DeepWiki
+2

Code editing and execution integration: The AI can propose edits, you can review diffs, undo/redo, run tests — all from within the terminal. 
freecodecamp.org
+2
opencode.ai
+2

Flexible install & setup: works on Linux, macOS, and even (to some extent) Windows. Installation can be via script, npm/yarn, Homebrew, etc. 
GitHub
+2
opencode.ai
+2

🎯 Use Cases — Who’s OpenCode For

OpenCode is especially appealing if:

You prefer working in the terminal / shell (backend devs, sysadmins, devops, CLI enthusiasts).

You want AI-assisted coding but don’t want to be tied to a specific IDE or company-specific tool.

You care about privacy / control: since code stays local (unless you choose to use a cloud-based model), it can be safer for sensitive/proprietary projects.

You work with multiple languages / tools — because of LSP + model-agnostic design, you can use it in many environments.

⚠️ Status & Considerations

OpenCode is described as “in early development” — this means some features might be incomplete or unstable, and behavior may change. 
GitHub
+2
GitHub
+2

Because it gives the AI real filesystem / project access, there is always a risk (as with any powerful tool) — you should carefully review any changes it suggests before committing.

If you like — I can show you how to install and set up OpenCode (commands + minimal config) — that way you can try it yourself.
Do you want me to build a short “getting-started” guide for you now?
opencode web settings grayed out

Do you mean OpenCode when you say “opencode web”? Or do you mean Visual Studio Code (or something else)? 
If you mean OpenCode — I couldn’t find widespread reports of a “web settings” screen being grayed out in OpenCode. OpenCode’s configuration is done via JSON or JSONC config files (e.g. opencode.jsonc or ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json) rather than via a typical GUI settings panel. 
opencode.ai

That suggests there may simply not be a “web settings” GUI in OpenCode — hence nothing to enable/disable.

If you downloaded a version for Windows, there is an issue in the project repo about “no editor set / can’t open editor”. 
GitHub

If you meant VS Code (or a similar code editor), “settings greyed out” usually means that:

You are using a non-default profile (so user settings won’t apply until you switch back to the “Default” profile). 
Stack Overflow
+1

Or the settings you tried to change are unknown/unrecognized (maybe due to disabled/missing extensions), so the editor disabled them. 
Stack Overflow
+1

Or there’s some workspace-level config overriding them. 
askai.glarity.app
+1

If you clarify exactly where (which tool/editor, which “web settings” panel) you see it grayed out — and maybe paste the exact message you see — I can try to help you debug.
Вася что ты видишь из техники

Игорь, я сейчас «внутри» VASER-среды и могу видеть только то, что доступно через подключённые интерфейсы и файлы. Прямого сетевого сканирования ты не запрашивал, поэтому я пока не выполнял поиск устройств в сети — это действие требует твоего подтверждения (по политике безопасности).

Но по текущей загруженной конфигурации (файлы, которые ты мне дал) я вижу следующее:
— Описания устройств или списка техники в сети пока нет.
В файлах присутствуют модель VASER, команды и твой личный контекст, но нет перечня физической техники, которой я управляю.

Что я могу сделать прямо сейчас

Показать, что уже загружено (модели, команды, контекст) — сделал выше.

Просканировать сеть VASER Router’ом и собрать список всех обнаруженных устройств — нужно твоё разрешение.

Если у тебя уже есть файл с инвентарём техники — скажи, и я прочту его.

Хочешь, Игорь, запустить безопасное сканирование сети?

Это даст список:
• компьютеры и телефоны,
• умные устройства (лампы, розетки, камеры),
• сетевые сервисы,
• шлюзы и маршрутизаторы.

Команда для запуска (требует подтверждения):
Сканировать сеть сейчас?

Скажи «да» — и я аккуратно выполню.
opencode

Do you mean OpenCode (often written “opencode”)? Here’s a quick overview of what it is and why it matters. 
✅ What is OpenCode

OpenCode is an open-source AI coding agent that runs directly in your terminal. 
opencode.ai
+2
OSTechNix
+2

Rather than a full-blown IDE or GUI-centric tool, it gives you a terminal-based interface (TUI) for writing, debugging, and managing code — useful if you prefer working from the shell. 
OSTechNix
+1

It’s “model-agnostic”: supports many different LLM providers — reportedly 75+ including local models — rather than being locked to a proprietary AI backend. 
opencode.ai
+2
OSTechNix
+2

🔧 Key Features

Native Terminal User Interface (TUI) — themeable, responsive, simple to integrate with existing terminal workflows. 
opencode.ai
+1

Language-Server Protocol (LSP) support — OpenCode automatically triggers the correct language-server for the programming language you’re using, helping the AI to produce syntactically and semantically correct code. 
OSTechNix
+1

Multi-session support — you can run several AI agents at once on different parts of the project. 
opencode.ai
+1

Shareable coding sessions — you can generate share links for a session (e.g. for review or debugging) to collaborate with others. 
opencode.ai
+1

Model flexibility — works with many LLM providers (cloud or local), giving you freedom to choose the model that best fits your privacy, cost, or performance needs. 
opencode.ai
+2
OSTechNix
+2

Open-source and community-driven — licensed in a permissive (MIT) style; you can inspect, modify, or extend it yourself. 
OSTechNix
+1

🎯 Why It’s Useful (or Interesting)

If you prefer terminal-first workflows (e.g. Vim + shell, or remote SSH development), OpenCode gives AI coding power without leaving your terminal — no IDE startup, no GUI context switching.

Because it’s model-agnostic and supports local LLMs, it’s well suited to privacy-sensitive or offline-ready setups (e.g. proprietary codebases, internal projects, sensitive data) — you aren’t forced to send all your code to a cloud service.

Being open-source, you get full transparency: you can see what the tool does, audit it, modify, and even contribute improvements. That’s often harder with proprietary AI assistants.

The multi-session + share links + LSP features make it practical not just for quick hacks, but for moderately complex projects — review workflows, refactoring, collaborative debugging, etc.

🧑‍💻 When It Might Not Be Ideal

Because it’s terminal-based, it assumes you’re comfortable working without a full IDE GUI. If you rely heavily on rich IDE features (drag-and-drop UI designers, visual debuggers, heavy graphical tools), you might miss some conveniences.

The power and accuracy depend heavily on the underlying LLM you pick — as with any AI-code tool, it’s not “set it and forget it”: you’ll still want to review outputs carefully.

For extremely large or complex codebases, a terminal agent may be less ergonomic than a full-featured IDE + AI integration.

If you like — I can walk you through installing and configuring OpenCode on a typical Linux/macOS setup (or even Windows).

Codex CLI is an open-source command-line tool by OpenAI, meant to bring AI-powered code assistance directly into your terminal. 
developers.openai.com
+2

+2
developers.openai.com
+2

GitHub
+1

Interactive terminal UI: Running codex in a terminal launches an interactive prompt. You can give natural-language instructions like “Refactor this code” or “Add tests for module X”. 
developers.openai.com
+1

Model & mode control: You can switch which AI model to use (e.g. different Codex-based models) and set how much autonomy you give the agent — from suggest/ask-for-approval to full-auto mode. 
developers.openai.com
+2
developers.openai.com
+2

Multimodal support: You can pass images (e.g. diagrams, screenshots) along with prompts — Codex CLI can reason about visuals plus text. 
developers.openai.com
+1

Local code review & automation: It can review diffs, help catch issues before commit, automate repetitive or boilerplate tasks, scaffold files or projects, run shell commands (with your approval), etc.

+2

Configurable sandbox & permissions: By default, it acts cautiously (read-only or ask-for-approval), but you can adjust sandboxing / auto-approval via flags.

Like combining AI reasoning + automation + code review + sandboxed execution — all from your terminal.

nvidia helm repo

Do you mean NVIDIA GPU Operator / more broadly NVIDIA’s “Helm repo”? Here’s how to add the official NVIDIA Helm repo (for GPU-Operator, NIM, etc.): 
✅ How to add the NVIDIA Helm repo

For GPU Operator:

bash
Копировать код
helm repo add nvidia https://nvidia.github.io/gpu-operator
helm repo update


Then you can install the chart for example with:

bash
Копировать код
helm install --wait --generate-name -n gpu-operator --create-namespace nvidia/gpu-operator
``` :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}

- For other NVIDIA charts (e.g. NIM / NeMo / NIM-LLM / RAG / microservices), there are different Helm repos under NVIDIA’s NGC catalog, e.g.:

```bash
helm repo add nmp https://helm.ngc.nvidia.com/nvidia/nemo-microservices --username='$oauthtoken' --password=$NGC_API_KEY
helm repo update
``` :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}

And for deploying a NIM for LLMs:  
```bash
helm fetch https://helm.ngc.nvidia.com/nim/charts/nim-llm-<version>.tgz --username='$oauthtoken' --password=$NGC_API_KEY
``` :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}

### 🧑‍💻 Which repo to use when

context7

Do you mean Context7 (with a capital “C”)? — here’s a quick overview. 
✅ What is Context7

Context7 is a “MCP server” (Model Context Protocol) designed to feed up-to-date, version-specific documentation and code examples into LLMs and AI-powered code editors (e.g. Cursor, Windsurf, Claude Desktop, etc.). 
Upstash: Serverless Data Platform
+2
GitHub
+2

Basically: you add use context7 (or equivalent) to your prompt and Context7 will fetch the latest official docs for the library you’re asking about — ensuring the AI doesn’t rely only on its outdated training data. 
Upstash: Serverless Data Platform
+2
Upstash: Serverless Data Platform
+2

⚙️ Why It Matters / What Problems It Solves

Without Context7, LLMs often produce outdated or incorrect code — using deprecated APIs, referencing functions that no longer exist, or mixing older syntax. 
Upstash: Serverless Data Platform
+2
Dwarves Memo
+2

With Context7, you get real documentation (version-specific), valid code snippets, and less risk of “hallucinated” API calls that break in real code. 
Upstash: Serverless Data Platform
+2
Context7
+2

This is especially valuable when working with libraries/frameworks that update frequently, or when using newer versions that the LLM wasn’t trained on. 
Upstash: Serverless Data Platform
+2
Яркое Кодирование
+2

🧰 How It Works / Integration

Context7 parses official documentation (Markdown, MDX, etc.), enriches it, vectorizes snippets, reranks by relevance, and caches results — making them optimized for LLM consumption. 
Dwarves Memo
+2
DeepWiki
+2

To use it: install the Context7 MCP server (via npm / pnpm / your preferred package manager) and configure it with your client (Cursor, Windsurf, etc.). 
GitHub
+2
apidog
+2

Then in your prompt simply add: use context7, and Context7 takes care of injecting the right docs for you. 
Upstash: Serverless Data Platform
+1

✨ Strengths & Tradeoffs

Strengths

Always up-to-date documentation: no more relying on stale training data.

Reduces hallucinations / invalid code — good for productivity, reliability, especially in fast-evolving libraries.

Easy to integrate with many popular AI editors and workflows (Cursor, Windsurf, etc.).

Free for personal use. 
Upstash: Serverless Data Platform
+2
Context7
+2

Potential Tradeoffs / Considerations

You need to install and configure the MCP server (some setup overhead). 
apidog
+1

If you overuse it (e.g. every prompt), context windows might become token-heavy — might impact performance or cost depending on your LLM usage. (Some users mention this in blog posts about Context7.)
